persons.arguments_against: The stint is single-initial 'J. Calder' and Calder is common (14 officials, 9 biographies), so the live counter-hypotheses are (a) a different J. Calder in North Borneo, or (b) a successor in the same post after James Calder left. Against (a): Chief Secretary of North Borneo is a unique, senior single-occupant office, and the biography states 'ch. sec., N. Borneo, 1946' with 'O.A.G. periods, 1946-48' immediately before the stint window; the stint holder also carries C.M.G., the bio subject's honour, in every year 1949-1952. Against (b): a successor sharing surname, initial J, AND the C.M.G. in the identical unique post starting the very year the bio (printed in the same 1949-1951 editions) still describes James Calder as the incumbent chief secretary would be an extraordinary coincidence; the bio's continued appearance through 1951 with no successor-shaped career break supports continuity. The link rests on specific position+place agreement plus a shared honour, not on the name.
persons.would_overturn: Staff-list or external evidence that James Calder left North Borneo before 1949 and a different J. Calder, C.M.G., succeeded him as Chief Secretary; or a death/retirement date before 1949.

notes: position_sim FAIL is an abbreviation artifact ('ch. sec.' = 'Chief Secretary'). The O.A.G. (Officer Administering the Government) periods 1946-48 explain why the chief-secretary staff listing may only begin at 1949 in this stint segmentation. Strong independent dimensions per the single-initial rule: exact unique-position+colony match and shared C.M.G. across all four stint years.
